Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
2008-02-29 2 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01 
1.0 INTRODUCTION 
1.1 Purpose 
The purpose of this Staff Instruction (SI) is to define Transport Canada Civil Aviation’s (TCCA) 
Level of Involvement (LOI) process in aeronautical product certification programs. 
1.2 Applicability 
This document is applicable to all TCCA aircraft certification personnel.  
1.3 Description of Changes 
This document is a new issue. 
2.0 REFERENCES AND REQUIREMENTS 
2.1 Reference Documents 
(1) It is intended that the following reference material s be used in conjunction with this document: 
(a) Part V Subpart 11 of the Canadian Aviation Regulations (CAR)— Approval of the Type 
Design of an Aeronautical Product; 
(b) Part V Subpart 13 of the CAR— Approval of Modification and Repair Designs;  
(c) Chapter 505 of the Airworthiness Manual (AWM)―Delegation Of Authority; 
(d) Chapter 511 of the AWM―Approval of the Type Design of an Aeronautical Product; 
(e) Chapter 513 of the AWM―Approval of Modification and Repair Designs; 
(f) Staff Instruction (SI) 511-005—Type Certification Process for Canadian Aeronautical 
Products;  
(g) SI 513-007—Approval of Domestic Modification and Repair Designs; and  
(h) Delegations Handbook for Designated Engineers and Design Approval 
Representatives—  
http://www.tc.gc.ca/civilaviation/certification/delegations/Handbook/menu.htm. 
2.2 Cancelled Documents 
Not applicable.   
2.3 Definitions and Abbreviations 
The following definition is used in this document:  
(a) Level of Involvement (LOI) means the process used to manage the participation of, and 
the activities undertaken by, TCCA specialists in the design compliance determination 
activities leading to the approval of aeronautical product type designs. 
(a) Authorized Person means an individual who performs delegated functions for an 
Approved Engineering Organization (AEO) or Design Approval Organization (DAO) on 
behalf of the Minister. 
3.0 BACKGROUND 
(1) A combined TCCA and industry working group recognized that improving t he clarity of the roles 
and responsibilities of the parties involved in type certification could improve the process. 
Fundamental principles of delegation were clearly identified and a more flexible method of 
managing TCCA participation in the type certification process was developed.  This new method 
facilitates efficient and effective participation of TCCA resources in the certification process and is 
known as the “Level of Involvement” (LOI) process.

title: PDF Page 3
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
(2) Applicants for the certification of new, modified or repaired type designs are responsible to TCCA 
for executing the agreed certification process with due consideration for LOI, and as such should 
be familiar with the contents of this SI. 
(3) Similarly, aircraft certification Delegates, including Authorized Persons, should also be familiar 
with the contents of this SI, as completion of the LOI process by TCCA specialists is required 
prior to issuing a design approval, whether or not the Delegate is issuing the approval. 
4.0 CERTIFI CATION PROCESS 
(1) SI 511-005 provides procedural guidelines concerning the planning and conduct of a type 
certification activity for a domestic aeronautical product.  These procedures identify four distinct 
phases of certification and include information on the phases where LOI is identified and 
accomplished.  This SI provides guidance on how to determine LOI content and provides further 
details of where it is implemented in the certification process. 
(2) Although SI 511-005 is modelled against a large domestic type certification activity, many 
elements of the domestic type certification process are adaptable to other projects, such as 
Supplemental Type Certificate (STC), whose processes tend to be subsets of elements contained 
in the generic large type certification project.  As such, an explanation of LOI and how it manifests 
itself in a large type certification project provides an accurate depiction of LOI in other types of 
projects, with a few exceptions that will be further explained.  
(3) SI 513-007 provides similar procedural guidelines for the approval of domestic modification and 
repair designs. 
5.0 LEVEL OF INVOLVEMENT  – THE INTENT 
(1) The LOI process is the method which will be used by TCCA to identify and manage the 
participation of TCCA specialists in the compliance demonstration activities conducted by an 
applicant for an aeronautical product type certificate or changes thereto.  It constitutes the TCCA 
contribution to the determination that an applicant’s type design complies with the product basis 
of certification and is an integral part of TCCA’s oversight responsibilities. 
(2) As a general principle, LOI is intended to increase flexibility while facilitating the participation of 
TCCA specialists in the risk sensitive compliance demonstration aspects of type certification 
programs.  Through the focused utilization of TCCA’s resources in these areas, the risk of an 
unsafe design is reduced while confidence is gained in the applicant’s ability to satisfactorily 
demonstrate compliance with design standards that are less risk sensitive.  The intended 
outcome, using this method and the Delegation of Authority as defined in Chapter 505 of the 
AWM, is that the Minister attains the highest confidence that the product meets Part V Subpart 11  
and Part V Subpart 13 of the CARs, and the referenced design standards. This, then, confirms 
that the applicant’s design is eligible for approval.  LOI criteria, primarily related to the risk 
sensitive factors of certification are identified later in this document.  
(3) The LOI process allows TCCA to be more selective about the elements of a certification program 
in which specialists will participate than was possible under the system it replaces.  For example, 
it is now feasible to identify a specific aspect of the compliance demonstration for a particular 
standard and focus the TCCA involvement accordingly, rather than having to undertake a review 
of all related compliance data as occurred in the past.  The flexibility inherent in the LOI will allow 
TCCA specialists to undertake whatever data review is necessary to support a finding of 
compliance, should this be necessary. 
(4) The LOI process complements TCCA’s adoption of risk management principles.

title: PDF Page 4
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
6.0 OVERVIEW OF THE IMPACT OF THE LOI PROCESS ON THE ROLES AND 
RESPONSIBILITIES OF PARTIES INVOLVED IN THE TYPE CERTIFICATION PROCESS 
Since the LOI process relates only to the role of TCCA specialists in the type certification 
process, the roles and responsibilities of other parties involved remain largely unchanged as 
discussed below. 
6.1 Applicants for Approval of Aeronautical Product Type Designs 
(1) Although the introduction of LOI will bring changes to the certification process, none of the 
applicant obligations are changed, including the responsibility to define the type design and 
demonstrate that it complies with the applicable standards of airworthiness. 
(2) To enable issuance of the type certificate the applicant must also provide, to the Minister, access 
to the product to conduct any inspection, engineering assessment and flight and ground tests 
necessary to determine compliance as specified in section 511.09 of the AWM.  Similar 
provisions exist in Part V Subpart 13 of the CARs for the approval of changes to type designs. 
6.2 Minister of Transport 
(1) TCCA specialists and delegates represent the Minister in the type certification process by 
undertaking the activities required in Part V Subpart 11 and Part V Subpart 13 of the CARs, which 
are discussed in more detail in section 9.0. The Minister’s representatives verify the applicant’s 
entitlement to the certificate by making findings of compliance (FOC).  Under the LOI system the 
delegate will make the FOC as per the authorized scope of delegation. 
(2) Type certificates and STCs are issued by TCCA, while delegates often issue Limited 
Supplemental Type Certificates (LSTCs) and other changes to the approved type design. The 
introduction of LOI does not impact the certificate issuance processes but will require the parties 
involved to ensure that  LOI is complete before the approval document is issued. 
6.2.1 Transport Canada Civil Aviation 
(1) TCCA will continue to have an active role in type certification projects in order to discharge its 
responsibilities under Part V Subpart 11 and Part V Subpart 13 of the CARs and the International 
Civil Aviation Organization.  The introduction of LOI is not intended to diminish or increase the 
involvement of TCCA in the certification process.   The type and extent of the activities 
undertaken will be determined in accordance with the LOI criteria detailed in section 7.0. 
(2) LOI impacts specific phases in the certification process, primarily the certification plan review 
phase, Phase Two, where LOI is specified, and the compliance demonstration phase, Phase 
Three, where LOI is conducted.  It is not part of, nor does it affect, other required type certification 
activities such as becoming familiar with the applicant’s design, developing the certification basis, 
reaching agreement on the means and methods of compliance, and issuing the approval 
document. 
(3) With the introduction of LOI, TCCA specialists will no longer make FOC where a delegate is 
available to do so.  However, TCCA specialists will still be expected to identify any concerns as 
soon as they arise, whether related to LOI activities or not.  Furthermore, specialists will also be 
requested to provide a recommendation to approve the type design or type design modification or 
repair on those programs where TCCA issues the approval document.  
(4) There will also continue to be programs where TCCA specialists are the sole representative of 
the Minister, that is, no delegate involvement.  In such instances, LOI activity must be sufficient to 
determine that the type design complies with the certification basis of the product.  
6.2.2  Delegation of Authority ― Chapter 505 of the AWM 
(1) Implementation of LOI does not change the roles and responsibilities of the delegate, however 
delegate procedures will require amendment to reflect the LOI process.  TCCA expectations 
related to making FOC and approving, or recommending approval of type design data remains 
2008-02-29 4 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01

title: PDF Page 5
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
unchanged. The delegate is responsible, within the authorized scope, for ensuring that the type 
design data has been examined and is complete and accurate and complies with the applicable 
airworthiness standards and procedural requirements.  The effectiveness of LOI as a risk based 
oversight function is dependent on the delegates continuing to conduct a complete review of all 
the applicant’s compliance data.  
(2) Delegates approve many changes to aeronautical product type designs without any direct 
involvement from TCCA.  The introduction of LOI will not change this practice. 
7.0 LOI CRITERIA 
(1)  LOI is determined using risk management principles. LOI will be greater where there is an 
increased risk of a product not being designed or certified properly. Because of the complexity of 
the certification process, the design of aeronautical products and the subjective nature of many 
design requirements, LOI cannot be established using a single approach.  LOI is established on a 
case-by-case basis. The level of activity is influenced by a number of risk factors or criteria 
associated with aeronautical product certification, including, but not limited to: 
(a) The characteristics of the product design - 
(i) Functio nal criticality; 
(ii) De sign complexity; 
(iii) Novelty of product or design change; and 
(iv) New or novel product manufacturing process.  
(b) The applicant’s capability: 
(i) Technical capability; 
(ii) Experience in the particular design/compliance approach; and 
(iii) Routine versus non-routine applicant project. 
(c) The delegate’s design / compliance knowledge:   
(i) Experience in the particular design/compliance approach; 
(ii) The delegate’s authorized scope of delegation; 
(iii) Delegate resources; and 
(iv) Past delegate performance. 
(d) The basis of certification:  
(i) New amendments, special conditions, equivalent safety findings, exemptions, 
foreign requirements; 
(ii) Need for subjective assessments to establish compliance; 
(iii) New or novel means and methods of demonstrating compliance; and  
(iv) Lack of adequate guidance material. 
(e) TCCA requirements:  
(i) Oversight of the delegate’s activities. 
(f) External factors: 
(i) Exceptional public interest; and 
(ii) Management of bilateral issues with other authorities. 
2008-02-29 5 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01

8.0 SPECIFICATION OF LOI 
(1) The table below illustrates examples of activities conducted during the compliance demonstration 
phase of a certification program and are typical LOI activities. 
(2) These activities are specified during the certification plan review or equivalent stage in the 
certification process. They may be specified against the design standards in large programs or for 
each deliverable in smaller projects.  In the context of the table, the term “Accept” means that the 
applicant should expect some form of feedback from TCCA as a result of the LOI element. This 
indicates that the element has been found acceptable as a contributor to the FOC to the 
standard(s) the element  addresses. 
Certification Plan Extent of 
Activity 
Activity 
Info 
only 
Review 
& 
Accept 
Witness/ 
Inspect & 
Accept 
1 Reviewing test plans (or Software Review Plans)    
2 Witnessing selected certification tests    
3 Conducting certification tests    
4 Reviewing test data and analysis    
5 Review compliance reports    
6 Conducting software process reviews    
7 Conducting engineering surveys or inspections    
8 Reviewing required documents and recommend for TCCA 
approval (i.e. AFM. AWL, MMEL) 
   
 
9.0 LOI IN THE CERTIFICATION PROCESS 
To furth er ill ustrate th e L OI pro cess, it s impleme ntation in th e fou r ph ases of a dom estic t ype 
certification project is discussed below. 
9.1 Phase One – Project Initiation and Basis of Certification 
(1) Phase One includes the initiation of the project, application for type certification by the applicant, 
initial product design familiarization by TCCA and foreign authorities where applicable, the 
submission of draft Certification Plans (refer to SI 500-015), and finally the Initial Type Board 
Meeting or equivalent.  There is no LOI conducted in Phase One, as activities related to the 
familiarization with the product design and the development of the basis of certification, via 
Changed Product Rule where applicable, are in fact necessary prerequisites for any TCCA 
defined LOI, and are accomplished regardless of the LOI that is eventually defined.    
(2) The two main outputs of Phase One are the agreed basis of certification and draft certification 
plans provided by the applicant.  The certification plans at this stage will consist of a description 
of the proposed means and methods of compliance against the basis of certification. These draft 
certification plans become the starting point for determination of acceptable means and methods 
of compliance with TCCA, as well as the basis upon which TCCA will specify its LOI, as 
discussed in Phase Two. 
2008-02-29 6 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01

title: PDF Page 7
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
9.2 Phase Two – Agree on Means and Methods of Compliance and Specification of LOI 
(1) Phase Two consists of a series of reviews and meetings aimed at seeking concurrence on the 
proposed means and methods of compliance.  As the compliance demonstration activities are 
established through these reviews, TCCA will specify it’s LOI, using LOI criteria defined above, 
and the applicant will record this LOI as part of the certification plan. The output of Phase Two is 
the agreed certification plans. The agreed certification plans define the applicant’s responsibility 
with regards to demonstration of compliance, that is part of Phase Three, and defines the 
activities where TCCA specialists will participate throughout this demonstration of compliance.  
(2) Documenting the LOI in the certification plan provides a clear definition of where TCCA will be 
involved in the applicant’s demonstration of compliance.  Procedures for tracking and recording 
completion of the LOI are detailed in SI 511-005.  
(3) It should be noted that although the LOI becomes a known quantity at the end of Phase Two, it is 
not necessarily a fixed quantity.  LOI is subject to change throughout the certification process for 
various reasons such as changes in design, failed tests, changes in means and methods of 
compliance and changes in scope of delegation, among others. As such, the LOI record system 
may be updated to reflect these changes. It is also emphasized that concerns identified by the 
TCCA specialists during the certification program must be addressed as early as possible, 
whether they are related to LOI or not, in order not to impact the TCCA specialists’ ability to 
recommend approval of the type design prior to issuance of the approval document in Phase 
Four. 
9.3 Phase Three – Demonstration of Compliance 
(1) Phase Three is simply the execution of the compliance demonstrations.  Phase Three is the 
phase where LOI is actually conducted.  As the certification demonstration progresses, LOI will 
be conducted and tracked by both the applicant and TCCA, and its completion recorded.   
(2) The demonstration of compliance by the applicant will be complete at the end of Phase Three. At 
this time, the following milestones will have been reached: 
¾ The applicant will be in a position to declare that he has demonstrated that the type 
design complies with Part V Subpart 11 and thus be in a position to request the issuance 
of the type certificate; 
¾ The delegate will have confirmed the applicant’s position by making FOC against all 
elements in the certification basis within his scope of authority, and; 
¾ The LOI will be declared satisfactorily complete by TCCA and  the FOC completed for 
those areas outside the delegate’s scope. 
9.3.1 Phase Three – Findings of Compliance  
(1) Section s 511.11 and 513.11 of the CARs stipulates that the Minister shall issue a certificate when 
the applicant has demonstrated that the product meets the applicable standards.  As such, 
compliance to the applicable standards shall be found by the Minister in order for the certificate to 
be issued.  Each delegate involved in an approval program is expected to make FOCs against all 
elements in the basis of certification within his/her defined scope of authority.   
(2) A delegate shall not make an FOC where he/she is aware of a disagreement with the TCCA 
specialist  accountable for the requirement in question.  On programs where the delegate has the 
authority to approve changes to an approved type design, and where TCCA has declared LOI, 
the delegate must confirm that the LOI has been satisfactorily completed before approving 
changes to the approved type design.  
(3) The General Compliance Plan, or equivalent, is typically used to record and track the FOC made 
by the delegate, and, when necessary, the TCCA specialists.  TCCA specialists will make FOCs 
only where there is a limitation of scope of authority of the delegate such as the: 
(a),  Airworthiness Limitations; 
2008-02-29 7 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01

title: PDF Page 8
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
(b)  Aircraft Flight Manual; and 
(c) Special Conditions Airworthiness. 
or where: 
(a) the delegate is unavailable to make the FOC; or  
(b) a delegate has not participated in the certification program. 
(4) In all cases the FOC is not considered part of the LOI, however it is recognized that where TCCA 
specialists are making FOCs, the LOI will be more extensive, as necessary, to find compliance. 
9.3.2 Completion of LOI 
 LOI must be complete for the issuance of a certificate.  A suitable tracking document will be used 
to record the completion of LOI, which was defined in Phase II and conducted in Phase III. 
9.4 Phase Four – Issuance of Type Certificate or Approval 
9.4.1 TCCA Issued Design Approvals 
Phase Four of the certification process concerns primarily the approval of the type design, 
including the Aircraft Flight Manual and airworthiness limitations and is largely unchanged by the 
introduction of LOI.  However, one new and important element is necessary in cases where the 
approval will be issued by TCCA.   This involves a formal recommendation by the TCCA 
specialists that the Minister approve the type design.  Also included in this activity will be 
agreement by the TCCA specialists that there are no known unsafe conditions that precludes 
issuance of the type certificate.   
9.4.2 Non -TCCA Issued Approvals 
In cases where the delegate has the authority to approve a design change to an approved type 
design, the delegate may issue the approval upon completion of any required FOCs, and also 
upon notification that TCCA has completed all of its declared LOI.  No other formal 
recommendation is required from TCCA. 
9.4.3 TCCA Specialist’s Role in Recommending Issuance of Approval (For TCCA Issued Design 
Approvals) 
In addition to the completion of LOI, TCCA specialists shall, as per SI 511-005 and SI 513-007, 
recommend the approval of the type design as far as their area of specialty is concerned.  This 
declaration will take the form of a signoff sheet appended to the Decision Record, recommending 
issuance of the approval, prepared by the Project Manager.  Despite the completion of their LOI, 
there may be outstanding concerns, outside the definition of the LOI per se, that need to be 
resolved.  For example, there may be cases where LOI has been declared complete some time 
prior to the completion of all activities previously defined as comprising the total demonstration of 
compliance to a requirement.  Despite the LOI being complete, the TCCA specialist, through, for 
example, continuing dialogue with the applicant or delegate counterpart, may be aware of an 
issue that would affect his/her ability to recommend the issuance of the approval/certificate.  The 
TCCA specialist, independent of LOI, is expected to make known at any time, and as soon as 
possible, any concerns potentially affecting the ability of the approval/certificate to be issued.  
Normally such concerns would be made prior to the final signoff being discussed here, but this 
final signoff would catch any remaining concerns during the final stages of the project. 
9.4.4 No Unsafe Features 
This refers specifically to section 511.11 of the CARs, provisions that give the Minister the 
discretion, despite compliance being demonstrated to the applicable standards, to withhold the 
approval if a feature or characteristic makes the product unsafe.  The TCCA specialist 
concurrence that there are no known unsafe conditions will be included in the sign-off activity  as 
stated in subsection 9.4.3. 
2008-02-29 8 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01

title: PDF Page 9
content:
Aircraft Certification Level of Involvement (LOI) 
10.0 SUPPLEMENTAL TYPE CERTIFICATES VERSUS TYPE CERTIFICATES – VARIATIONS IN 
PROCESSES 
(1) The sections above describe how the LOI process manifests itself in the certification process, but 
more specifically in a process typical of a large domestic type certification program, usually 
managed by National Aircraft Certification.  Although some of the process elements mentioned 
above may not exist in the same form for STC projects managed by a TCCA Regional Office, the 
same principles still apply in as much as the definition and philosophy of LOI is concerned.  The 
formality of terms such as Certification Plans, LOI Matrices, Type Board Meetings, certification 
phases, among others, may not be strictly used in all cases in STC/LSTC projects, but often there 
will exist equivalent documents and events that represent the equivalent concept.  Of importance 
in this document is the understanding and application of the LOI process, which can be applied 
regardless of the exact process leading to the issuance of an aeronautical product approval. 
(2) One variation in the generic certification process that exists in the Regional context is where the 
delegate can issue the certificate, such as an LSTC.  There may be such cases in which the 
approval is issued when there has been no contact with TCCA whatsoever.  In other cases, 
where the delegate issued the certificate, TCCA was consulted on the means and methods of 
compliance and the basis of certification but no LOI was involved.  Also possible is the situation 
where the delegate issued the certificate but TCCA participated in the development of the basis 
of certification, discussed and agreed to the means and methods of compliance used, and did 
also  conduct specified LOI. 
11.0 IMPLEMENTATION 
(1) The LOI process has been successfully evaluated on a number of programs and has been 
implemented with some applicants/delegates on a pilot basis.  The results of the trial programs 
have been good and increases in efficiency have been realized.   Implementation of LOI will be 
proceeding, as a result, on all programs and projects.   
(2) Implementation of LOI will require changes in the certification process and procedures on the part 
of applicants and delegates.  Specifically changes to the Delegate’s Design Approval Procedures 
Manual, the Letters of Authorization for Designated Engineers, Compliance Program and other 
documentation will be required.  In addition, applicants are expected to track completion of the 
LOI activities.   Further details on the required changes will be provided in the future.  
Implementation can proceed in the interim on a case-by-case basis in consultation with the 
National Aircraft Certification, Project Management Division. 
2008-02-29 9 of 10 SI 500-003 Issue 01
